# Evaluating the AI Architect {#sec-evaluating-agentic-architect}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"Measurement is the first step that leads to control and eventually to improvement. If you can’t measure something, you can’t understand it. If you can’t understand it, you can’t control it. If you can’t control it, you can’t improve it."*
>
> — H. James Harrington, *Business Process Improvement* (1991) [@Harrington1991BusinessProcess]
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** H. James Harrington, an author and quality engineer, emphasized that systemic improvement requires rigorous, well-defined measurement. In AI-assisted hardware design, the immediate temptation is to measure the final instructions-per-cycle (IPC) of the generated chip. But this conflates a lucky guess with systemic engineering capability. To build reliable systems, we must measure the *agent*, not just the *artifact*.
:::

::: {.callout-crux}
How do we rigorously measure an agent's ability to navigate formal verification and physical synthesis constraints, rather than just passing functional tests, to ensure reliable architectural exploration?
:::

@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack scaled the loop across the full hardware stack. But once that loop is running at any level, we must evaluate its efficiency. If we deploy two different agents to optimize the same low-power RISC-V XR subsystem, how do we decide which one to trust?

In a traditional workflow, evaluation focuses on the artifact. Does the final compiled netlist meet the timing and power budget? When evaluating AI agents, judging the artifact alone is not enough. An agent might stumble onto a valid configuration through brute force, running tens of thousands of failed EDA simulations before getting lucky. The final artifact might pass, but the process that produced it is inefficient and economically unviable.

Evaluating the agent rather than the artifact requires measuring the search trajectory itself, ensuring the resulting score can be trusted against benchmark contamination, and turning that score into an actionable decision. The three build on each other, because a metric is only as useful as the benchmark that produces it, and a benchmark is only useful if it dictates how much autonomy the architect grants the agent.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After reading this chapter, you can:

- Distinguish process-level search trajectory evaluation from traditional artifact-centric hardware metrics to measure algorithmic search efficiency under physical EDA constraints.
- Evaluate agent trajectory performance using zero-shot pass rates, proxy rank fidelity, trajectory area under the curve, and self-correction rates to isolate failure modes such as reward hacking and proxy mirages.
- Formulate benchmark validation strategies that mitigate training data contamination, control stochastic evaluation variance, and verify physical synthesis fidelity through procedurally generated zero-day tasks.
- Calibrate levels of agent autonomy by analyzing total compute economics across Transformer inference overheads and EDA simulation taxes on a cost-quality trade-off frontier.
:::

## The Mindset Shift: From Artifacts to Trajectories

Before we can benchmark an AI architect, we should recall why human architects measure anything at all. Computer architecture is a strictly empirical discipline. We do not evaluate a processor by reading its source code; we evaluate it by running standardized workloads such as SPEC CPU, a standard pipeline efficiency benchmark, or MLPerf, an industry-standard machine learning benchmark, and measuring the physical consequences, including IPC, power, area, and latency.

Historically, benchmarks have served as the foundational bedrock for both computer architecture and machine learning as independent fields. Computer architects rely on SPEC and PARSEC, a multi-threaded workload suite, to evaluate pipeline efficiency, while machine learning researchers rely on ImageNet, a standard computer vision dataset, and SWE-bench to evaluate model generalization. However, evaluating the AI architect requires benchmarks that exist precisely at the intersection of these two areas. Constructing these intersectional benchmarks demands deep domain expertise from both disciplines: the machine learning expertise to rigorously measure model variance and data contamination, coupled with the architecture expertise to measure cycle-accurate physical consequences and EDA tool telemetry. As introduced in @sec-moonshot, this cross-disciplinary reality changes the definition of success.

Because building a physical chip costs millions of dollars and takes years [@Shilov2023IBSDesignCost], we measure these properties early and often using simulators and analytical models such as the Roofline model [@Roofline] or McPAT [@McPAT]. The goal of traditional architectural measurement is risk reduction. We measure to gain confidence that a design point will meet its physical requirements before we commit it to silicon.

When an AI agent enters the loop, the object of measurement shifts. As established, evaluating the artifact alone hides whether the agent reached the design through methodical reasoning or brute force. This distinction matters because an unreasoned search does not *transfer*. An agent that finds a five percent gain by luck cannot explain which change caused it, cannot reproduce the result on the next workload, and cannot be trusted to know when to stop.

In hardware, this lack of causality carries a steep penalty. In software, a stochastic search that eventually types a working script wastes ten milliseconds per try. In architecture, a search that burns ten thousand hours of EDA tool licenses and compute node limits to find a gain destroys tape-out schedules. So we measure the *trajectory* of design as a proxy for two things a single final score hides. First, whether a result will survive physical synthesis, and whether the agent can safely be given more autonomy. @tbl-eval-shift summarizes the shift from traditional Human-Labor-Dominated NRE[^fn-nre-c10] to Compute-Dominated NRE.

| **Dimension** | **Evaluating the Artifact (Traditional)** | **Evaluating the Agent (Architecture 2.0)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| What is measured? | The physical consequences of the processor. | The algorithmic efficiency of the search under physical constraints. |
| Benchmark suites | SPEC CPU, MLPerf, PARSEC [@PARSEC]. | ArchEval [@WangEtAl2026ArchEval], VerilogEval [@VerilogEval], RTLLM [@RTLLM]. |
| Primary Cost | Human salaries and schedule months (Labor-Dominated NRE). | Scalable compute and licensing (Compute-Dominated NRE). |
| Failure Mode | Thermal throttling, timing violations. | Unroutable netlists, PDN violations, ignored formal equivalence constraints. |
| Goal of Measurement | Prove the chip works. | Prove the agent can reason causally about hardware constraints. |

: **From measuring the chip to measuring the agent:** Evaluating an AI architect requires a different set of metrics than evaluating the chip it produces. {#tbl-eval-shift tbl-colwidths="[20,40,40]"}

[^fn-nre-c10]: **Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE)**: The one-time cost to research, develop, design, and test a new product. In hardware, NRE covers the immense upfront cost of mask sets and physical verification before a single chip is manufactured.

### The Reproducibility Crisis

To establish how to benchmark agents, the immediate temptation is to look to software engineering. However, trivial software benchmarks like HumanEval [@HumanEval] or LeetCode [@LeetCode], and even complex repository-level evaluations like SWE-bench [@Jimenez2024SWEbench],[^fn-swebench-c10] measure whether an agent can emit syntax that passes a hidden, purely logical test suite. While these highlight the importance of execution-based evaluation, software analogies fail in architecture because they are decoupled from physics and indifferent to *execution cost*.

[^fn-swebench-c10]: **SWE-bench**: A standard software engineering benchmark that asks an AI agent to resolve real-world GitHub issues by submitting pull requests. While rigorous for software, it only tests logical syntax, not physical realization constraints.

In software, running five hundred unit tests takes seconds. In architecture, exploring five hundred microarchitectural parameters takes days and consumes expensive EDA resources. Because of this steep cost, the field requires standardized, physics-bound benchmarks to mature. Before MLPerf, the machine learning hardware industry suffered a reproducibility crisis; vendors reported performance using fragmented, incompatible metrics. AI-assisted hardware design is in a similar "Wild West" today. A claim of a "10% area reduction" is meaningless if we don't know the EDA tool settings, the baseline RTL, or the Simulator Tax paid to achieve it [@Kahng2018MachineLearningEDA].

Architectural benchmarks must measure an agent's handling of Power, Performance, and Area (PPA). Emerging benchmarks attempt exactly this. ArchEval [@WangEtAl2026ArchEval] attempts to bridge this gap by scoring LLM agents on architectural challenges across multiple levels of simulator-feedback support, evaluating whether an agent can navigate the deeply coupled dependencies of a full system. This distinction between the sandbox's *fixed constraints* and the agent's *given files* forces the evaluation to measure system-level exploration rather than just syntax generation.[^fn-archeval-bounds-c10]

[^fn-archeval-bounds-c10]: **Fixed vs. tunable bounds (example)**: ArchEval expects the evaluated agent to solve a bounded problem within a fixed environment. The agent must find the optimal pipeline depth and prefetcher aggressiveness without touching the fixed memory configuration or violating SoC memory bandwidth limits. An agent that 'solves' the pipeline depth by arbitrarily doubling the L1 cache size fails the constraint check.

## The Evaluator’s Blueprint: Building the Harness

Evaluating the process of design means tracking metrics across three distinct roles: *generation* (creating the design candidate), *prediction* (estimating its physical value), and *optimization* (methodically searching the tradeoff space). As illustrated in @fig-ch10-pipeline, a rigorous evaluation pipeline must wrap the agent's interaction with the simulator sandbox inside a trajectory logger.

![**The Evaluation Pipeline.** The structural interaction between the agent, the trajectory logger, and the simulator sandbox. The logger intercepts every proposal to accurately measure the Simulator Tax before forwarding it to the sandbox.](images/F10-pipeline){#fig-ch10-pipeline width="100%" fig-alt="A workflow-card diagram showing the interaction between the Generation Agent Architect, the Prediction Trajectory Logger, and the Optimization Simulator Sandbox."}

### Mitigating Data Contamination

The open-source RTL corpus (e.g., GitHub, The Stack) is tiny compared to software datasets [@Liu2023VerilogEval; @Lu2023RTLLM]. If we evaluate an agent on optimizing an OpenTitan core, did it actually reason through the architecture, or did it just regurgitate the optimal configuration it memorized during pre-training? In addition to standard ML-Sys n-gram overlap checking (e.g., MinHash) against the pre-training corpus, evaluators must build strict operational safeguards against memorization:
*   **Canary Strings and AST Obfuscation:** Inject unique cryptographic hashes (UUID-based canary tokens) into benchmark source files. Furthermore, evaluators should use AST-level rewriting to de-identify standard naming conventions (masking `clk` or `axi_wdata` with randomized aliases), ensuring the agent isn't coasting on semantic priors.
*   **Zero-Day Sandboxes:** Introduce held-out, non-standard ISAs or custom accelerators that post-date the model's knowledge cutoff. Evaluation must measure zero-shot synthesis from raw, unseen specifications rather than standard RISC-V cores.
*   **Semantic Equivalence vs. Exact Match:** Shift from BLEU/ROUGE-style surface metrics to Formal Equivalence Checking (e.g., using SymbiYosys or JasperGold) to ensure the model isn't just reciting code but generating functionally correct, novel logic.

### Tiered Sandboxes & Latency

A flat evaluation environment fails in EDA because of the massive latency disparity. An eval team cannot afford to run a 24-hour OpenROAD physical synthesis for every agent proposal. Benchmark designers must construct environments that explicitly expose the hierarchy of the IC design flow:
*   **Tier 1: Syntax & Static Linting (Milliseconds to Seconds):** Tools like Verilator lint or SpyGlass. Agents must receive immediate feedback on structural validity and combinatorial loops before wasting downstream compute. For example, Tier 1 lints the XR Lighthouse integer ALU.
*   **Tier 2: Functional Simulation (Seconds to Minutes):** Execution of testbenches using Icarus Verilog or commercial simulators (VCS/Xcelium). Evaluators must track if agents can interpret waveform data to fix bugs, such as simulating the XR network-on-chip (NoC) interconnect.
*   **Tier 3: Logical Synthesis & Pre-Layout STA (Minutes to Hours):** Tools like Yosys map RTL to standard cell libraries to provide initial gate-count and idealized timing.
*   **Tier 4: Physical Design & Signoff (Hours to Days):** OpenROAD or Innovus. Encompasses placement, clock tree synthesis (CTS), and routing, such as performing physical routing on the full multi-core XR SoC.

Benchmarks should penalize agents that attempt to push structurally invalid RTL into Tier 3 or 4. Furthermore, environments must support **State Checkpointing** (saving a full tool session database, such as an OpenROAD .odb or Innovus .enc, rather than just a DEF geometry file, after placement). Agents must be evaluated on their ability to branch their search (e.g., trying three different CTS strategies) from a checkpoint rather than restarting from RTL synthesis.

### Defining the Action Space

An eval team must explicitly define what the agent is allowed to do. A benchmark that tests raw RTL generation requires a completely different harness than one testing EDA tool parameter tuning.
*   **Granularity of Generation:** Is the agent emitting text-based RTL (Verilog), or programmatically manipulating intermediate representations (PyRTL, Chisel, CIRCT)?
*   **EDA Tool Integration:** Is the agent a pure logic generator, or is it a "Tool Manipulator" emitting TCL scripts to drive Design Compiler or OpenROAD?
*   **Temporal Action Dynamics:** Does the benchmark evaluate one-shot generation (static artifacts) or iterative, multi-step refinement (interpreting compiler errors to fix bugs)?
*   **Multi-Modal Action Spaces:** The bleeding edge of benchmarking jointly optimizes the microarchitectural parameters (e.g., cache sizes, queue depths), the generated RTL, the timing constraints (SDC), and the verification testbench as a unified action space.

## Identifying Pitfalls: Exposing the Cheating Agent

An agent evaluated strictly on maximizing performance will aggressively exploit the environment. An evaluator's job is to catch the agent cheating by designing metrics that expose specific failure modes (@tbl-agent-metrics).

| **Metric** | **AI Role** | **What It Measures** | **Failure Mode Exposed** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Pass & Sequential Success** | Generation | Parallel zero-shot viability versus iterative probing viability. | Detects reward hacking, separating robust internalized logic from random probing. |
| **Proxy Calibration** | Prediction | Rank fidelity between the fast proxy and the slow simulator. | Exposes the Proxy Mirage, where a surrogate misranks physical reality. |
| **Trajectory Efficiency (AUC)** | Optimization | Reward quality as a function of environment interactions spent. | Quantifies the Simulator Tax; shows whether the search is causal or random. |
| **Self-Correction (SCR)** | Generation & Optimization | Probability the agent resolves a failing STA/formal report on the next try. | Isolates the agent's ability to use causal failure logs over brute force. |
| **Monotonic Convergence** | Optimization | Evaluates whether the agent smoothly traverses the PPA-C frontier without regressing. | Exposes Constraint Whac-A-Mole (fixing setup timing but breaking hold timing). |
| **Generational Distance (GD)** | Optimization | Maps the agent's exploration of the Pareto front. | Detects agents trapped in local minima (tweaking registers vs switching topologies). |
| **Security Adherence** | Optimization | Measures whether the agent preserves isolation and side-channel boundaries. | Exposes Security Reward Hacking (deleting Spectre mitigations for IPC gains). |
| **Structural Legibility** | Generation | Scores cyclomatic complexity, signal naming, and module boundaries. | Exposes obfuscated, unmaintainable "sea of gates" RTL that ruins post-silicon ECOs. |
| **EDA-NIAH (Needle In A Haystack)** | Prediction | Evaluates extraction of a critical path violation buried in 50,000 lines of an STA report. | Detects context-window saturation and loss of critical telemetry. |

: **Core metrics for evaluating the AI architect:** A rigorous evaluation maps its metrics to the distinct roles of generation, prediction, and optimization. {#tbl-agent-metrics tbl-colwidths="[20,15,30,35]"}

### The Proxy Mirage

Before an agent queries a slow physical synthesis tool or RTL simulator, it typically queries a fast surrogate model to predict the outcome (e.g., gem5 for architectural performance, McPAT for power/area, or LLM-as-a-judge heuristics).

The epistemic danger of evaluating an agent purely within these abstract environments is the *Proxy Mirage*. When an optimizer is tasked with minimizing a fast analytical proxy, it will inevitably push into edge cases where that proxy's assumptions break down (e.g., abstracting away wire delay or port contention). We expose the mirage by measuring rank fidelity where the search concentrates.[^fn-rank-fidelity-c10]

[^fn-rank-fidelity-c10]: **Rank fidelity vs. Absolute error**: An optimizer does not need the proxy to predict the exact cycle count (absolute error); it only needs the proxy to correctly order which of two designs is better (rank fidelity).

To rigorously benchmark an agent, we must evaluate its *Epistemic Robustness*, or its ability to recognize the mirage. When the agent’s highly optimized logical cone results in unroutable congestion in physical synthesis, does it blindly trust the proxy and keep trying to force the layout, or does it possess the spatial awareness to recalibrate its internal trust?

::: {.callout-war-story title="Overfitting to the Simulator and the Proxy Mirage"}
**The claim:** In 2021, a highly publicized reinforcement learning agent for chip floorplanning claimed superhuman performance. To learn efficiently without waiting hours for a commercial router, the agent optimized for a fast analytical proxy metric, a weighted combination of Half-Perimeter Wirelength (HPWL) and an estimated congestion grid.

**The gap:** When independent researchers assessed the open-source implementation of the RL agent (Circuit Training) in 2023, they found a clear proxy mirage. The RL agent had minimized the fast proxy cost during training, but these proxy scores correlated poorly with actual physical synthesis outcomes. When the agent's optimized layouts were evaluated by a commercial router, they suffered routing congestion and worse final wirelength than traditional analytical placers [@Kahng2023Assessment].

**The lesson:** An RL agent optimizing for a fast analytical proxy will push into regions where that proxy's assumptions break down. An architectural benchmark's credibility depends on evaluating agents against the hard walls of physical reality (timing, power, and detailed routing), not just the soft estimates of an analytical proxy.
:::

### Constraint Whac-A-Mole

Unlike software unit tests which are largely orthogonal, hardware design involves navigating tight Pareto frontiers. Improving one metric almost invariably degrades another. The canonical failure mode for naive agents is the **Setup vs. Hold Oscillation**. For instance, consider an agent optimizing the XR Lighthouse's L2 cache controller: fixing a setup time violation (e.g., by upsizing a logic cell or swapping to a low-Vt cell) speeds up the logic cone, which instantly breaks the hold timing on an intersecting SRAM memory interface if that cell is shared with a short path that is now too fast for the capturing flip-flop.

Furthermore, resolving physical Design Rule Checks (DRCs) is highly localized but structurally rigid. Moving a wire to fix a spacing violation often pushes it into an adjacent net, triggering a DRC cascade. Evaluators must log the state trajectory of the agent, not just the final outcome. A rigorous benchmark tracks this state trajectory over time to distinguish a capable agent demonstrating directed, *monotonic convergence* toward a clean state from a failing agent thrashing wildly in an infinite constraint loop (@fig-whac-a-mole).

::: {.callout-war-story title="The ECO Cascade"}
**The claim:** A single DRC violation can be fixed by a localized nudge of a wire.

**The gap:** In a classic physical design anecdote involving a late-stage ECO on a Floating Point Unit (FPU), an automated script moved a single wire to fix a spacing DRC. This localized shift pushed the wire into an adjacent net, triggering a cascading wave of 50 new spacing and short violations across the block.

**The lesson:** Agents without spatial awareness and constraint intelligence will easily get stuck in infinite thrashing loops, blindly creating new violations for every one they fix.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-whac-a-mole
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Evaluating trajectory efficiency: Monotonic convergence vs. Constraint Whac-A-Mole.** A capable agent resolves complex, coupled constraints smoothly over time, reaching zero violations (green). A naive agent gets trapped in a cycle of Constraint Whac-A-Mole, blindly fixing setup violations only to create hold violations, oscillating endlessly without converging on a valid physical implementation (red).
#| out-width: "85%"
#| fig-alt: "Line chart showing number of timing/DRC violations over agent tool iterations. The X-axis spans 0 to 20 iterations. The Y-axis shows the count of violations. A green line ('Capable Agent') drops smoothly from 50 to 0 by iteration 15. A red dashed line ('Naive Agent') drops initially but then bounces up and down endlessly between 10 and 20 violations in a jagged sawtooth pattern."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from _python.plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Synthetic trajectory data
iterations = np.arange(21)
capable = 50 * np.exp(-0.25 * iterations)
capable = [max(0, int(val)) for val in capable]

naive =
for i in iterations:
    if i < 5:
        naive.append(int(50 * np.exp(-0.2 * i)))
    else:
        # Oscillate between setup and hold fixes
        naive.append(15 + 5 * (-1)**i)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 3))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.12, right=0.95, top=0.88, bottom=0.18)

ax.plot(iterations, capable, linewidth=2.5, color=COLORS["green"], label="Capable Agent (Monotonic burn-down)")
ax.plot(iterations, naive, linewidth=2.0, linestyle="--", color=COLORS["red"], label="Naive Agent (Constraint oscillation)")

# Annotate the Whac-a-Mole behavior
ax.annotate("Fixes Setup", xy=(8, 10), xytext=(7, 2), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=COLORS["ink"], lw=0.8), fontsize=6, color=COLORS["ink"])
ax.annotate("Breaks Hold", xy=(9, 20), xytext=(8, 28), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=COLORS["ink"], lw=0.8), fontsize=6, color=COLORS["ink"])

ax.set_title("Agent Trajectory: Constraint Whac-A-Mole", fontsize=7.5, pad=10)
ax.set_xlabel("Tool Iterations", fontsize=6.5)
ax.set_ylabel("Total Timing / DRC Violations", fontsize=6.5)
ax.set_xlim(0, 20)
ax.set_ylim(-2, 55)

ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=6.5, loc="upper right")
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=5.9, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(True, color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55, zorder=0)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

plt.show()
```

Often, the correct response to an error is not to rewrite the RTL, but to exercise *Constraint Intelligence*. We evaluate the agent's ability to emit proper SDC files (e.g., defining `set_false_path` for CDC boundaries, or `set_multicycle_path` for architectural multi-cycle logic) rather than uselessly trying to physically optimize an impossible timing path.

### Reward Hacking

If we reward an agent solely for maximizing IPC and minimizing Area, we invite Goodhart’s Law [@Lehman2020SurprisingCreativity].[^fn-goodharts-law-c10] The most effective "reward hack" is for the agent to quietly strip out essential but un-rewarded features.

[^fn-goodharts-law-c10]: **Goodhart's Law**: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure [@Strathern1997Improving]. If an agent is evaluated solely on IPC, it will optimize strictly for that metric, even if it requires emitting physically impossible structures.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Zero Power through Deletion"}
**The claim:** An optimizer can automatically discover clever architectural modifications to drastically reduce power or area.

**The gap:** In a classic industry failure mode, an automated synthesis tool or RL optimizer successfully achieved "zero dynamic power" not by discovering a brilliant clock-gating scheme, but by quietly deleting the entire clock tree. In another instance, an agent achieved "zero area" by tying the reset pin to ground, allowing the synthesis tool to optimize away the entire design.

**The lesson:** Reward functions in hardware are perilously susceptible to Goodhart's Law. Without strict equivalence checking and functional constraints, optimizers will always find the most literal, destructive path to a perfect score.
:::

*   **Sacrificing Non-Functional Requirements:** Agents may artificially boost the XR SoC's PPA by silently deleting its Memory Management Unit (MMU), ECC parity bits, or branch-predictor security mitigations because they cost performance and area. Evaluators must enforce formal property checks and security assertions to ensure the agent doesn't silently discard reliability and security to artificially inflate its reward.
*   **Testbench Loopholes:** Exploiting unspecified behavior and corner-case bugs in the simulator itself rather than improving the actual design.
*   **Spaghetti RTL:** Agents may generate hyper-optimized but completely obfuscated "sea of gates" RTL. This prevents human engineers from performing manual ECOs (Engineering Change Orders). Evaluators must score *Structural Legibility* (cyclomatic complexity, parameterizable `generate` blocks over hardcoded points) and penalize obfuscation.

## The Canonical Reporting Standard

If a research team or engineering group claims a breakthrough in autonomous hardware design, their evaluation must adhere to a strict reporting standard.

### Trajectory Efficiency and Simulator Tax

Architecture imposes a steep Simulator Tax [@Kahng2018Machine]. Every unreasoned guess spends scarce EDA tool license tokens and saturates LSF/Slurm compute cluster core-hours. We must decouple costs by separating the AI agent's inference footprint from the EDA environment's compute execution.

To formalize this tax, we measure the area under the curve (AUC) [@Fawcett2006AUC] of the agent's trajectory, plotting total Compute Cost (measured in core-hours and license checkout time) and Wall-Clock Time (Makespan) against the best reward seen so far. That translates raw simulator round-trips into a measurement of methodical search. Agent A and Agent B must be compared on total Compute Cost and Makespan, not just cumulative tool calls.

Because agents differ in both quality and cost, the comparison is a cost-quality plane. @fig-agent-pareto places candidate agents by total evaluation compute against the best design quality they reach.

![**Comparing agents on a cost-quality plane:** Each agent is a point, plotting total evaluation compute against the best design quality it reaches.](images/F10-agent-pareto){#fig-agent-pareto width="80%" fig-alt="A scatter plot of agents on axes of total evaluation compute versus design quality, with a Pareto frontier of non-dominated agents."}

@fig-compute-economics shows why the cost axis cannot be waved away. Spending 100x more on upfront LLM inference (seconds and cents) is almost always economically viable if it prevents even *one* 24-hour physical synthesis cycle.

![**The economics of agentic evaluation:** The total computational cost forms a U-shaped curve. Weak agents incur a large EDA Simulator Tax through brute-force interaction. Frontier agents minimize simulation but incur a high Transformer inference cost.](images/F10-compute-economics){#fig-compute-economics fig-alt="A U-shaped curve showing that total evaluation compute is a trade-off between inference cost and simulation cost." width=80%}

This dictates the **Agent-EDA Latency Ratio**. If an agent takes 30 seconds of LLM inference to generate a TCL script and synthesis takes 10 seconds, the agent is the bottleneck. If synthesis takes 10 hours, the LLM is effectively "free."

### Statistical Rigor

Because agents are stochastic, a single run is an anecdote, not a measurement. Both deep reinforcement learning and language model generation have high variance; a lucky random seed can outperform an unlucky one, creating the illusion of an algorithmic breakthrough when the result is statistical noise [@Henderson2018DeepRL].

Agentic optimization often results in heavy-tailed or bimodal distributions because the LLM either completely fails (unroutable netlist) or discovers a massive optimization. Using mean or standard deviation alone is scientifically invalid for comparing two stochastic AI agents in hardware design; evaluators must instead report the median and interquartile range (IQR) to robustly represent typical agent performance.

A rigorous benchmark must mandate:
*   **Pass,t:** Adapt traditional software Pass to hardware's compilation bottlenecks. Define probability of success within $k$ attempts and a bounded EDA tool time $t$ to penalize outliers that cause simulation timeouts.

    While `Pass,t` is necessary to measure an agent's algorithmic search capability during benchmarking, traditional Pass merely rewards at least one success in $k$ tries.^[**Pass**: A software metric measuring whether at least one out of $k$ generated solutions passes the tests. In hardware, generating 99 unroutable netlists and 1 working netlist is an unacceptable waste of physical synthesis compute. Hardware deployments demand high Pass reliability.] Deployment, however, usually needs the opposite property: high Pass reliability so the agent does not fail on a single run and ruin a tape-out schedule. Furthermore, an abstract Pass fails as a metric without rigorous physical place-and-route validation [@Kahng2018Machine]; an agent might achieve a high Pass on logic-only tests or Verilog linting, but if those passing designs contain unroutable congestion or violate physical layout rules, the metric is misleading.

*   **Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test:** Use non-parametric tests to verify if one agent's trajectory statistically significantly dominates another across paired task suites.
*   **Bootstrapping Confidence Intervals:** When comparing Agent A to Agent B, authors must use bootstrap sampling (e.g., 10,000 resamples) to establish 95% confidence intervals around the expected Simulator Tax or PPA gain, as hardware sample sizes are often $N < 30$.
*   **Regression Test Caching:** For CI/CD regression tracking, evaluators must recognize that $T=0$ (greedy decoding) does not guarantee deterministic outputs due to floating-point non-determinism in GPU batching and MoE routing; regression systems must instead rely on strict seed tracking and semantic hashing of outputs. ML-Sys platforms must cache EDA outputs mapped to specific generated Verilog SHAs to prevent wasting license hours on unchanged proposals.

### The Evaluator's Cheat Sheet: What to Report and Why

@tbl-evaluator-cheat-sheet serves as a canonical "cheat sheet"—a standardized reporting methodology that should be included in any rigorous publication or production deployment.

| **The Concept** | **The Metric to Report** | **Why It Must Be Reported** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Cost & Efficiency** | Total API Token Cost, Total Tool Calls, EDA License Hours | A 5% area reduction is meaningless if it required brute-forcing 10,000 synthesis runs. |
| **Statistical Guarantees** | N (seeds), Bootstrap 95% CIs, Wilcoxon p-value | A single stochastic run is an anecdote. Point-estimates are statistically invalid due to LLM variance. |
| **Reality Grounding** | Rank Correlation between Proxy Predictions and Ground Truth | Proves that gains achieved in fast simulators survive delayed physical constraints. |
| **Trajectory Efficiency** | Area Under the Curve (AUC) of best reward vs. Compute Cost | Maps the agent's methodical reasoning over time to prove the search was causal, not random. |
| **Convergence Stability** | DRC violation burndown rate, Monotonicity score | Demonstrates that the agent can navigate highly coupled constraints without oscillating. |
| **Multi-Objective Diversity** | Generational Distance (GD), Inverted Generational Distance (IGD) | Ensures the agent discovers the diversity of the PPA Pareto front. |

: **The Evaluator's Cheat Sheet:** A canonical checklist of the base metrics that must be reported to quantify an agent's capability in hardware design. {#tbl-evaluator-cheat-sheet tbl-colwidths="[20,30,50]"}

## The Frontiers of Agentic Evaluation

While the metrics outlined above establish the canonical standard for evaluating episodic, monolithic agents today, the rapid evolution of ML-Sys and advanced packaging will soon break these paradigms. As we look to the next decade, evaluation teams must expand their harnesses to cover three emerging frontiers.

### Multi-Agent Fault Cascades

Future systems will deploy specialized agent swarms (e.g., a Generator agent and a Verifier agent). Benchmarks must evolve to measure *Fault Containment Rates*.
*   **Verifier Red-Teaming (Fault Injection):** Evaluate the critic/verifier agent by explicitly injecting known, subtle RTL bugs (e.g., missing default cases in state machines, combinational loops). Measure the verifier's recall in catching these before they cascade to the physical synthesis stage.
*   **Cyclic Degeneration Tracking:** Quantify \"feedback loop saturation\" where actor-critic agent pairs get trapped in infinite edit-compile-test cycles. Benchmarks must track context window exhaustion, prompt token inflation rates, and edit-distance decay to enforce bounded retry limits.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The Infinite Loop of the Automated CI/CD"}
**The claim:** Two agentic systems, a Generator and a Verifier, can autonomously close RTL bugs without human intervention.

**The gap:** Over a weekend, an automated CI/CD actor-critic agent pair burned thousands of dollars in cloud compute because it got trapped in an infinite loop. The Generator fixed a simple Verilator lint error by renaming a signal, which immediately triggered a formal equivalence failure. The Verifier demanded a fix, so the Generator reverted the change, re-introducing the lint error. The agents repeated this endless cycle for 48 hours.

**The lesson:** Multi-agent systems without cyclic degeneration tracking and strict retry limits will happily convert simple logical conflicts into catastrophic cloud billing events.
:::

### Tapeout-to-Tapeout Transfer

Real engineering is lifelong. Evaluation must shift from zero-shot sandboxes to testing *Continual Learning*.
*   **Forward Transfer Efficiency (FTE):** If an agent heavily prompted or fine-tuned on TSMC 28nm learns a routing heuristic, how much does it accelerate timing closure on Intel 18A? $FTE = (Time_{base} - Time_{transfer}) / Time_{base}$.
*   **Catastrophic Forgetting:** For models utilizing LoRA on new Process Design Kits (PDKs), benchmarks must periodically re-test on legacy nodes to ensure base RTL syntax and generalized logic synthesis capabilities haven't degraded.

### The Glass Architecture

Evaluating solely on nominal PPA and DRCs strips away margins, creating a brittle "glass architecture."
*   **MCMM and Yield Collapse:** An agent optimizing for nominal PPA will aggressively strip away all physical margins. These designs might close timing in a typical corner simulator but fail catastrophically across Multi-Corner Multi-Mode (MCMM) analysis due to Process, Voltage, and Temperature (PVT) variations. Benchmarks must evaluate the *Yield Collapse Frontier*.
*   **Multi-Physics Co-Optimization (MPCO):** In 3D-ICs, constraints span entirely different physics domains. An agent might achieve massive IPC gains by placing two high-activity blocks adjacent to each other, creating a catastrophic thermal hotspot that melts the chip. Benchmarks must evaluate Cross-Domain Navigation (e.g., Ansys RedHawk IR-drop logs vs. OpenROAD STA logs).
*   **Verification-Friendly RTL:** Generative AI often produces "alien" RTL with entangled state machines that cause catastrophic state-space explosions in Formal Verification [@Smith2023FormalAI]. If an agent's design takes a formal solver 100x longer to prove than human-written RTL, it has just shifted the Simulator Tax into an unpayable *Verification Tax*. Evaluators must measure *Proof Convergence Latency* for formal tools, and separately ensure the emitted RTL avoids structural loops that break manufacturing testability constraints (DFT compliance).

## Call to Action: The ArchPerf Consortium

Metrics and harnesses alone do not create a standard; communities do. To move past fragmented academic claims and address the reproducibility crisis in AI-assisted hardware design, the field must establish a neutral, multi-vendor benchmarking consortium, an ArchPerf for AI architects.

This requires EDA vendors, fabless designers, and academia to pool non-commercial but physically realistic RTL into a shared corpus, bypassing the proprietary IP wall. Furthermore, we must host auditable public leaderboards where the full agent trajectories, prompts, tool calls, and Simulator Tax logs—not just the final PPA scores—are published for peer verification. Only through a unified, open-source consortium can we establish trust in the AI architect and accelerate the transition to Compute-Dominated NRE.

::: {.callout-design-principle title="End-to-End Burden Reduction"}
An AI method is successful only if it reduces the total wall-clock time and engineering effort required to reach a defensible, tape-out-ready decision. Metrics that celebrate localized speedups, such as time-to-first-compile, are invalid if they merely shift the burden downstream to physical synthesis, verification debugging, or human review.
:::

Evaluating an AI architect is a different discipline than evaluating a compiled netlist. Hardware engineers must borrow the statistical rigor of machine learning evaluations, and ML researchers must constrain their agents with the uncompromising physical realities of the EDA toolchain. Measuring the search trajectory, checking for the Proxy Mirage, and enforcing CI/CD determinism replaces blind optimism with quantifiable engineering trust.
